questions.question: What does the capstone build, and why that task?
options: A small decoder-only transformer trained end-to-end on character-level Shakespeare — laptop-trainable in under 10 minutes, yet correct enough that a bigger dataset and longer training yields a real LM
questions.explanation: It's the course's nanoGPT — the shape of Karpathy's reference implementation, retooled around the thirteen preceding lessons. Reading papers isn't the same as making the pieces work together.

questions.question: Which 2026-modern primitives does the capstone block use?
options: RMSNorm (pre-norm placement), causal multi-head attention, residual connections, and a SwiGLU FFN — with the lm_head weight-tied to the token embedding
questions.explanation: Each piece maps back to a lesson: norm and FFN from lesson 05, the causal mask from 07, positions from 04 (with a RoPE option). The skeleton is the same one frontier models refine.

questions.question: Why tie the lm_head to the token embedding?
options: It's required for the softmax to work
The input embedding maps token → vector and the output head maps vector → token logits — sharing one matrix for both saves V×d parameters and acts as a regularizer; standard in GPT-class models
questions.explanation: For a small model the embedding can be a large fraction of all parameters, so tying is nearly free quality — the two mappings are natural transposes of each other.

questions.question: What training objective drives the capstone?
options: Shift-by-one cross-entropy from lesson 07: inputs predict the next character at every position, all positions trained in parallel under the causal mask
questions.explanation: Same loss as every GPT — character-level vocabulary just makes the pipeline small enough to inspect end to end.

questions.question: What is the pedagogical point of a from-scratch build when libraries exist?
options: Integration is where understanding gets tested: configs, initialization, masking, loss wiring, and generation loops hide the bugs papers never mention — and a working small model proves each lesson's piece actually composes
questions.explanation: "Thirteen lessons. One model. No shortcuts." — the difference between knowing the blocks and having made them run.
